hint: Opposites attract — find the part of the DNA molecule that carries a constant negative charge along the backbone.

q: In a molecular drawing of DNA, which structural feature forms the two outer 'rails' of the ladder?
options: The sugar-phosphate backbone
The hydrogen bonds between paired bases
The stacked nitrogenous bases
The major and minor grooves
explain: The alternating sugar-phosphate backbone runs along the outside of each strand, forming the rails, while the hydrogen-bonded base pairs form the rungs in the interior.
hint: The rails run continuously down each strand; the bases form the rungs in between.

q: On a three-dimensional model of the DNA double helix, what are the major and minor grooves?
options: The two ends of each strand, labeled 5′ and 3′
The larger and smaller gaps that spiral along the outside of the helix
The two strands of the sugar-phosphate backbone
The hydrogen bonds linking complementary bases
explain: As the two antiparallel strands wind around each other, they create a wider major groove and a narrower minor groove spiraling along the helix surface. These grooves are gaps, not the backbone rails or the base-pair rungs.
hint: These features are the uneven indentations that wind around the helix where proteins can read the bases.

q: Which DNA conformation is right-handed, about 2.4 nm in diameter, and has its base pairs tilted away from the central helical axis?
options: Single-stranded DNA
A-DNA
Z-DNA
B-DNA
explain: A-DNA is a right-handed helix that is wider (~2.4 nm) and more compact than B-DNA, with base pairs tilted relative to the axis. B-DNA is the standard ~2.0 nm form, and Z-DNA is left-handed.
hint: It's still right-handed like the common form, but shorter, wider, and with tilted bases — favored under dehydrating conditions.

q: Which description matches B-form DNA, the predominant conformation under physiological conditions?
options: A right-handed helix that is short and wide with tilted bases
A right-handed helix with roughly 10 base pairs per turn
A left-handed helix with a zigzag backbone
An unwound single strand with no helical turns
explain: B-DNA is the normal right-handed double helix with about 10 base pairs per turn. The left-handed zigzag describes Z-DNA, and the short, wide, tilted-base form describes A-DNA.
hint: This is the 'textbook' Watson–Crick helix; count roughly ten rungs for each full twist.

q: Which statement accurately describes the structural arrangement of the two strands in a DNA double helix?
options: The strands are identical copies running in the same direction
Both strands run 3′ to 5′ in the same direction
The strands are antiparallel, one running 5′→3′ and the other 3′→5′
The strands run parallel, both oriented 5′ to 3′ in the same direction
explain: The two strands are antiparallel: one runs 5′→3′ while its complement runs 3′→5′, with purine-pyrimidine pairs between them. Parallel, same-direction, or identical strands would not allow proper complementary base pairing.
hint: Picture the two backbones pointing in opposite directions, like cars in adjacent lanes of opposing traffic.

q: What is the complementary strand of 5′–TGACGTGAT–3′, written in the 3′→5′ direction aligned beneath the template?
options: 3′–TCACGCAGT–5′
3′–TGACGTGAT–5′
3′–ACTGCACTA–5′
3′–ATCACGTCA–5′
explain: Pairing each base of 5′–TGACGTGAT–3′ (A↔T, G↔C) gives the antiparallel complement 3′–ACTGCACTA–5′. The other choices either copy the template or reverse the order incorrectly.
hint: Pair A with T and G with C base-by-base, keeping the antiparallel strand aligned directly underneath (no reversal).

q: What is the complementary strand of 5′–ATTGCTACGG–3′, written in the conventional 5′→3′ direction?
options: 5′–ATTGCTACGG–3′
5′–TAACGATGCC–3′
5′–GGCATCGTTA–3′
5′–CCGTAGCAAT–3′
explain: The complement of 5′–ATTGCTACGG–3′ is 3′–TAACGATGCC–5′; rewritten 5′→3′ it reads 5′–CCGTAGCAAT–3′. The 5′–TAACGATGCC–3′ choice gives the complement bases without reversing the direction.
hint: First make the antiparallel complement, then flip it so you can report it 5′→3′.

q: What is the complementary strand of 5′–GTATCTGCCA–3′, written in the conventional 5′→3′ direction?
options: 5′–ACGGTCTATG–3′
5′–TGGCAGATAC–3′
5′–CATAGACGGT–3′
5′–GTATCTGCCA–3′
explain: The complement of 5′–GTATCTGCCA–3′ is 3′–CATAGACGGT–5′; written 5′→3′ this is 5′–TGGCAGATAC–3′. The 5′–CATAGACGGT–3′ choice lists the complement bases without reversing direction.
hint: Build the antiparallel partner with A–T and G–C, then reverse it to express the sequence 5′→3′.

q: In DNA, by what type of bond are successive nucleotides covalently linked along a single strand?
options: 2′–5′ glycosidic bonds
3′–5′ phosphodiester bonds
Hydrogen bonds between adjacent sugars
Peptide bonds between phosphate groups
explain: Adjacent nucleotides are joined by 3′–5′ phosphodiester bonds connecting the 3′ hydroxyl of one sugar to the 5′ phosphate of the next. Hydrogen bonds join the two strands across the helix, and peptide bonds belong to proteins.
hint: The backbone links the 3′ carbon of one sugar to the 5′ phosphate of the next through bridging oxygens.

q: A DNA molecule is twisted so that it is either overwound or underwound relative to its relaxed B-form state. This condition is best described as which of the following?
options: Supercoiled
Annealed
Denatured
Methylated
explain: Over- or underwinding relative to the relaxed state introduces torsional strain that the molecule relieves by coiling upon itself, which is supercoiling. Denaturation is strand separation, annealing is re-pairing, and methylation is a base modification — none describes altered twist.
hint: Think about the strain stored in a tightly twisted phone cord that coils back on itself.

q: Relaxed B-form DNA averages one helical turn per ~10 bp. A 100-bp DNA segment is found to contain 15 complete helical rotations. How is this segment best classified?
options: Relaxed
Positively supercoiled
Negatively supercoiled
Single-stranded
explain: A relaxed 100-bp duplex holds ~10 turns; 15 turns means it is overwound (more turns than relaxed), which defines positive supercoiling. Fewer turns than relaxed would be negative supercoiling, the opposite case.
hint: 100 bp relaxed should hold about 10 turns; count whether 15 is too many or too few.

q: In a living cell, supercoiling of DNA is generated primarily by which of the following processes?
options: Rotation of the helix during replication and transcription
Random thermal motion of free nucleotides
Covalent crosslinking of adjacent thymines
Spontaneous base tautomerization
explain: As polymerases unwind and track along the helix during replication and transcription, they force the helix to rotate, generating torsional strain that manifests as supercoiling. Tautomerization causes point mutations, and thymine crosslinking is UV damage — neither produces supercoils.
hint: Picture machinery tracking along and unwinding the double helix as it works.

q: Which statement correctly distinguishes positive from negative supercoiling of DNA?
options: Positive is overrotation (overwinding); negative is underrotation (underwinding)
Positive involves only purines; negative involves only pyrimidines
Positive occurs in prokaryotes; negative occurs only in eukaryotes
Positive is underrotation; negative is overrotation
explain: Positive supercoiling reflects overwinding (more helical turns than relaxed), whereas negative supercoiling reflects underwinding (fewer turns). The base-composition and organism-specific options are fabricated distinctions.
hint: More twist than relaxed versus less twist than relaxed — match the sign to the direction.

q: Compared with the bacterium E. coli, which organism contains roughly 35 times as much DNA per cell?
options: Tobacco mosaic virus
Fruit fly (Drosophila)
Bacteriophage lambda
Another strain of E. coli
explain: The fruit fly, a eukaryote, carries roughly 35 times the DNA per cell of E. coli. Viruses such as phage lambda and tobacco mosaic virus have far smaller genomes than a bacterium, not larger.
hint: Think of a multicellular eukaryote with a much larger genome than a bacterium.

q: Which phrase best characterizes how DNA is arranged within eukaryotic cells?
options: Freely floating as loose linear strands
Permanently fixed and inert
Tightly packed and dynamic
A single naked circular molecule
explain: Eukaryotic DNA is wrapped with proteins into chromatin that is both tightly packed and dynamically remodeled to allow access. A single naked circle describes a typical bacterial chromosome, and the 'fixed/inert' or 'free-floating' options contradict chromatin's regulated, condensed organization.
hint: Consider that eukaryotic DNA must compact enormously yet still open up for transcription.

q: Chromatin in regions containing actively transcribed genes is generally characterized as which of the following?
options: More relaxed and more methylated
More condensed and more methylated
More relaxed and more acetylated
More condensed and more acetylated
explain: Active genes sit in chromatin that is more relaxed (open) and more acetylated, since histone acetylation neutralizes positive charge and loosens DNA–histone contacts. Condensation and heavy methylation are hallmarks of silenced chromatin instead.
hint: Adding acetyl groups loosens histone grip; transcription needs the DNA accessible.

q: Which pairing correctly classifies heterochromatin and euchromatin?
options: Heterochromatin = dense and inactive; euchromatin = loose and active
Heterochromatin = loose and inactive; euchromatin = dense and active
Both are equally condensed and equally transcribed
Heterochromatin = loose and active; euchromatin = dense and inactive
explain: Heterochromatin is densely packed and transcriptionally inactive, while euchromatin is loosely packed and actively transcribed. The other pairings swap or scramble the density-versus-activity relationship.
hint: The prefix 'eu-' means true/good — link it to the openly expressed state.

q: Which sequence correctly orders the levels of DNA packaging from least to most condensed?
options: Protein wraps around chromatin to form DNA, which condenses into a chromosome
DNA wraps around protein to form chromatin, which condenses into a chromosome
Chromosome wraps around DNA to form chromatin, which condenses into protein
Chromatin wraps around chromosomes to form DNA, which condenses into protein
explain: DNA wraps around histone proteins to form chromatin, which further condenses into a chromosome. The other orderings invert this hierarchy, e.g., wrongly making DNA the most condensed end product.
hint: Start with the thread (DNA), then the beads-on-a-string, then the fully packed structure.

q: The primary (most basic) structural level of DNA in the cell is best described as which of the following?
options: The condensed metaphase chromosome
A nucleosome bead-on-a-string array
The 30 nm chromatin fiber
Double-stranded B-form DNA
explain: The most basic structural level is the double-stranded B-form helix; all higher-order packaging (nucleosomes, 30 nm fiber, chromosome) is built upon it. The other choices are progressively higher levels of compaction, not the primary structure.
hint: Before any wrapping or folding, what is the bare double helix itself?

q: The nucleosome core particle around which DNA is wound is composed of which set of histone proteins?
options: Two copies each of H1, H2A, H3, and H4
Two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4
Four copies each of H3 and H4 only
One copy each of H1, H2A, H2B, and H3
explain: The nucleosome core is a histone octamer containing two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4. H1 is the linker histone that sits outside the core, not part of the octamer, so options including H1 in the core are wrong.
hint: Count to eight: the core is an octamer made of two of each core histone.

q: In the classic textbook model of chromatin packing, which level of organization is described as predominating in interphase chromatin?
options: Naked, unwrapped B-form DNA
The 30 nm fiber
The fully condensed metaphase chromosome
The 10 nm bead-on-a-string fiber only
explain: In the classic hierarchical model, nucleosomes (the 10 nm 'beads on a string') coil into a ~30 nm fiber that predominates during interphase, while the fully condensed form appears at metaphase; nuclear DNA is never left naked. (Whether a discrete 30 nm fiber exists in vivo is debated in current research, but it is the expected answer in the standard textbook model.)
hint: Between divisions DNA is compacted but not maximally — one step beyond beads-on-a-string.

q: Regarding DNA packaging in eukaryotic cells, which of the following statements is correct?
options: Fully extended DNA is shorter than the diameter of the nucleus
Mitotic chromosomes are in their least condensed state
Chromosomes consist of DNA combined with protein
Chromosomes exist in cells as long stretches of naked B-DNA
explain: Chromosomes are nucleoprotein complexes of DNA plus histone and non-histone proteins. The distractors are each false: DNA is not stored as naked B-DNA, fully extended DNA is far longer than the nucleus is wide, and mitotic chromosomes are the most (not least) condensed.
hint: Eukaryotic chromosomes are never bare helix — what partners with the DNA?

q: A diploid human cell contains 6.4 billion bp of DNA, with each nucleosome covering ~160 bp of core DNA plus a 40-bp linker (~200 bp per repeat). Using ~9 histones per nucleosome (the octamer plus H1), approximately how many histone molecules are in the cell?
options: About 288 million histones
About 200 thousand histones
About 6.4 billion histones
About 32 million histones
explain: About 6.4 billion bp ÷ ~200 bp per nucleosome ≈ 32 million nucleosomes, and 32 million × 9 histones ≈ 288 million histones. The 32-million figure is the nucleosome count (before multiplying by histones per nucleosome), not the histone total.
hint: Divide total bp by ~200 to get nucleosomes, then multiply by 9 histones each.

q: Using a per-nucleosome footprint of a 147-bp core plus ~22 bp of H1-associated DNA plus a 35-bp linker, a diploid human genome of ~6.4 billion bp yields approximately how many nucleosomes and histone proteins?
options: About 31,000,000 nucleosomes and 280,000,000 histones
About 64,000,000 nucleosomes and 576,000,000 histones
About 310,000,000 nucleosomes and 2,800,000,000 histones
About 3,100,000 nucleosomes and 28,000,000 histones
explain: With ~204 bp per nucleosome repeat (147 + 22 + 35), 6.4 billion bp gives ≈ 31 million nucleosomes, and ~9 histones each gives ≈ 280 million histone proteins. The other counts are off by an order of magnitude or use the wrong repeat length.
hint: Add 147 + 22 + 35 ≈ 204 bp per repeat, divide the genome by that, then ~9 histones each.

q: Using a per-nucleosome footprint of a 147-bp core plus ~22 bp of H1-associated DNA plus a 40-bp linker, a diploid human genome of ~6.4 billion bp yields approximately how many nucleosomes and histone proteins?
options: About 1,600,000 nucleosomes and 14,000,000 histones
About 288,000,000 nucleosomes and 31,000,000 histones
About 31,000,000 nucleosomes and 62,000,000 histones
About 31,000,000 nucleosomes and 280,000,000 histones
explain: With ~209 bp per nucleosome repeat (147 + 22 + 40), 6.4 billion bp gives ≈ 31 million nucleosomes, and ~9 histones each gives ≈ 280 million histones. Swapping the two counts or using ~2 histones per nucleosome produces the incorrect options.
hint: Repeat length ≈ 147 + 22 + 40 ≈ 209 bp; divide the genome, then ~9 histones per nucleosome.

q: Which structures are located at the physical ends of eukaryotic chromosomes?
options: Telomeres
Centromeres
Nucleolar organizer regions
Origins of replication
explain: Telomeres are the repetitive DNA-protein structures that cap the ends of linear eukaryotic chromosomes; centromeres, by contrast, lie internally and serve as the spindle attachment point.
hint: Think of the protective 'caps' on shoelace tips, at the very ends of each chromosome.

q: What is the primary function of the enzyme telomerase?
options: It relieves supercoiling ahead of the replication fork
It removes RNA primers from Okazaki fragments
It seals nicks in the sugar-phosphate backbone
It lengthens telomeres and prevents chromosome shortening
explain: Telomerase is a reverse transcriptase that uses its internal RNA template to add telomeric repeats, lengthening telomeres and counteracting the end-replication problem; nick sealing is ligase's job and supercoiling relief is topoisomerase's.
hint: It carries its own RNA template to extend the very ends and offset end-replication loss.

q: A single-stranded, G-rich 3′ overhang is a defining structural feature of which chromosomal region?
options: The telomere
The centromere
An internal coding exon
The replication origin
explain: Telomeres terminate in a single-stranded G-rich 3′ overhang that tucks back to form a protective t-loop; centromeres and origins are not characterized by such overhangs.
hint: Where does the G-rich strand (e.g., TTAGGG repeats) run past its complement to form a protective tail?

q: Among the sequences TTAGGG, TTGGGG, TTAGGC, and CCGGGG, which one is NOT a known telomeric repeat?
options: CCGGGG
TTAGGG
TTAGGC
TTGGGG
explain: CCGGGG is not a known telomeric repeat. TTAGGG is the human/vertebrate repeat, TTGGGG occurs in ciliates like Tetrahymena, and TTAGGC is the nematode (Caenorhabditis) telomere repeat.
hint: Authentic telomere repeats are G-rich on the 3′ strand; spot the one whose pattern breaks the mold.

q: Which feature best characterizes highly repetitive DNA?
options: Short sequences repeated in tandem many times
Sequences transcribed into mature mRNA
Single-copy regulatory promoter elements
Long unique genes present in a single copy
explain: Highly repetitive DNA consists of short sequences (e.g., satellite DNA) repeated in tandem hundreds to millions of times; protein-coding and single-copy regulatory sequences are not highly repetitive.
hint: Think satellite DNA: very short motifs stacked head-to-tail, not protein-coding genes.

q: Which statement about telomeres is INCORRECT?
options: Telomeres protect chromosome ends from degradation and fusion
Telomeres tend to shorten with successive somatic cell divisions
Telomeres consist of short tandem repeats added by telomerase
Telomere length is generally uniform across different chromosomes and cells
explain: Telomere length is not uniform; it varies among chromosomes, cells, and individuals and changes with cell division. The other statements—tandem repeats added by telomerase, end protection, and progressive shortening—are all true.
hint: One claim assumes a fixed, uniform length—telomere length actually varies.

q: What is the most likely fate of a chromosome that loses its centromere?
options: It replicates twice as fast and is overrepresented
It fails to attach to the spindle, does not segregate properly, and is usually lost
It is converted into a circular plasmid and retained
It fuses end-to-end with neighboring chromosomes
explain: An acentric chromosome lacks the kinetochore-assembly site, so spindle microtubules cannot attach; it fails to segregate into a daughter nucleus and is typically lost. End-to-end fusion is what happens when telomeres (not centromeres) are lost.
hint: No centromere means no kinetochore—so nothing for the spindle fibers to grab during division.

q: What is the principal function of the centromere?
options: It serves as the attachment point holding sister chromatids together
It encodes the histone proteins for packaging
It initiates DNA replication of the chromosome
It caps and protects the chromosome ends
explain: The centromere is the constricted region that holds sister chromatids together and assembles the kinetochore for spindle attachment; protecting chromosome ends is the telomere's role, not the centromere's.
hint: It is the constricted region where the kinetochore forms and sister chromatids stay joined until anaphase.

q: What is the name of the constricted region that physically links two sister chromatids?
options: Centromere
Telomere
Replication origin
Chromomere
explain: The centromere is the region where sister chromatids remain joined until anaphase; telomeres cap the ends, and the replication origin is where DNA synthesis begins, not a chromatid-linking site.
hint: Same structure that the spindle grabs—located where the two chromatids stay joined.

q: If the shelterin complex were defective, how would a cell most likely treat its telomeres?
options: As origins of replication to be fired early
As centromeres recruiting kinetochore proteins
As single-stranded RNA to be degraded
As double-strand breaks to be repaired
explain: Shelterin caps telomeres so the DNA-damage machinery ignores the natural chromosome end; without it, the cell perceives the end as a double-strand break and attempts repair, causing end-to-end fusions. It would not be read as an origin or a centromere.
hint: Shelterin's job is to disguise the natural chromosome end so the cell does not mistake it for DNA damage.

q: Beyond telomerase, what additional activity do somatic cells use to regulate telomere length?
options: Mechanisms that both trim and elongate telomeric sequences
Methylating telomeres to permanently fix their length
Converting telomeric DNA into ribosomal RNA
Inserting centromeric repeats at the chromosome ends
explain: Somatic cells regulate telomere length through processes that both trim (e.g., telomere rapid deletion) and elongate telomeric sequences, not just telomerase-driven extension; centromeric repeats and rRNA conversion are unrelated to telomere maintenance.
hint: Length control is bidirectional—cells can shorten as well as lengthen the repeat tract.

q: Which statement about telomeres is FALSE?
options: Telomeres protect chromosome ends from degradation and fusion
Telomeres consist of tandemly repeated, G-rich sequences
Telomeres are found universally in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes
Telomeres can be extended by the enzyme telomerase
explain: Telomeres are characteristic of linear eukaryotic chromosomes; typical prokaryotes have circular chromosomes with no ends to cap, so they lack telomeres. The other statements—G-rich tandem repeats, end protection, and telomerase extension—are all true.
hint: Telomeres solve the end-replication problem of LINEAR chromosomes—what shape are typical bacterial genomes?

q: Based on their abundance, eukaryotic genomic DNA is conventionally divided into which three classes of sequences?
options: Unique-sequence DNA, moderately repetitive DNA, and highly repetitive DNA
Euchromatin, heterochromatin, and constitutive heterochromatin
Coding DNA, intronic DNA, and intergenic DNA
Satellite DNA, minisatellite DNA, and microsatellite DNA
explain: Eukaryotic DNA is grouped by copy number into unique-sequence (single/few-copy), moderately repetitive, and highly repetitive DNA. Coding/intron/intergenic describes function and location, not repetition, and satellite/mini-/microsatellite are all subtypes of repetitive DNA rather than the three broad classes.
hint: The classification is by how many copies of a sequence are present in the genome, not by whether it codes for protein.

q: The C-value of an organism is defined as which of the following?
options: The number of protein-coding genes in the genome
The haploid amount of DNA in the nucleus of a cell
The total diploid DNA content of a somatic cell
The fraction of the genome that is repetitive DNA
explain: The C-value is the haploid nuclear DNA content of a species. It is a measure of total DNA mass, not gene number (which it correlates poorly with), and it is haploid rather than diploid.
hint: Think 'C' for the constant, haploid (one chromosome set) quantity of nuclear DNA.

q: Which statement accurately describes the relationship between C-value and organismal complexity in eukaryotes?
options: C-value is directly proportional to the number of protein-coding genes
More complex organisms invariably have larger C-values than simpler ones
There is no strong correlation between C-value and organismal complexity among eukaryotes
C-value increases linearly with the number of cell types in an organism
explain: Among eukaryotes, genome size (C-value) does not track organismal complexity; some morphologically simple organisms have enormous genomes. Thus claims of a strict proportionality to complexity, cell-type number, or gene count are all false.
hint: Some amoebae and plants have far more DNA than humans do.

q: The C-value paradox refers to which observation?
options: Haploid cells contain twice the DNA of diploid cells
Identical organisms can differ in haploid DNA content
Genome size does not correlate with organismal complexity
Gene number greatly exceeds the number of expressed proteins
explain: The C-value paradox is that genome size (haploid DNA content) shows no consistent relationship with organismal complexity, largely because much of a genome is noncoding/repetitive DNA. The mismatch between gene number and protein number is a separate idea (the G-value or N-value paradox).
hint: The 'paradox' is that bigger genomes don't mean a more sophisticated organism.

q: Which type of environment is generally most favorable for the long-term preservation of ancient DNA in skeletal remains?
options: A waterlogged peat bog
A dry, arid cave
A shallow, sun-exposed riverbed
A warm, humid tropical forest
explain: Dry, cool, stable conditions such as an arid cave minimize hydrolysis and microbial activity, preserving DNA best. Warmth, humidity, water, and sunlight all accelerate DNA breakdown.
hint: DNA degradation is slowed by cold, dry, and stable conditions—think low moisture and protection from sunlight.

q: In the genomic analysis of the ancient hominin nicknamed Denny, what finding established that the individual was female?
options: Two copies of every autosome but no sex chromosomes
The presence of two X chromosome sequences
A single X and a single Y chromosome sequence
The absence of any mitochondrial DNA
explain: Detecting two X-chromosome sequences (XX) and no Y showed Denny was female. A single X with a Y would indicate a male, and autosomes are present in both sexes.
hint: Recall the chromosomal basis of biological sex: which sex-chromosome combination lacks a Y?

q: What present-day genetic evidence indicates that early Homo sapiens and Denisovans interbred?
options: Traces of Denisovan DNA in present-day human genomes
Denisovan fossils found alongside modern human tools
A complete absence of archaic DNA in modern humans
Identical mitochondrial genomes in all living humans
explain: Surviving Denisovan-derived sequences in the genomes of some present-day human populations are direct evidence of past interbreeding. Shared fossils or tools are archaeological, not genetic, and a total absence of archaic DNA would argue against admixture.
hint: Interbreeding leaves a lasting genetic signature—look for archaic sequences surviving in living people.

q: Which finding indicated that Denisovans and Neanderthals had interbred somewhere in Denny's deeper ancestry?
options: Denny carried two identical Denisovan X chromosomes
Denny's parents were both purely Neanderthal
Denny's mitochondrial DNA was entirely Denisovan
Neanderthal DNA detected within Denny's Denisovan father's contribution
explain: Stretches of Neanderthal DNA found within the genome contributed by Denny's Denisovan father reveal Neanderthal-Denisovan interbreeding generations earlier. Denny herself was a first-generation hybrid (Neanderthal mother, Denisovan father), so this deeper signal had to come through the paternal Denisovan line.
hint: Denny's mother was Neanderthal and father Denisovan; deeper interbreeding would show as the 'other' lineage's DNA inside one parent's contribution.

q: Patients with an inherited mitochondrial muscle disease were examined for deletions in their mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA). What pattern did the deletions show across affected individuals?
options: Only unaffected relatives showed mtDNA deletions
All affected individuals shared a single identical deletion
Affected individuals lacked mitochondrial DNA entirely
There was no common deletion; affected bands appeared at different positions
explain: Affected individuals carried multiple different mtDNA deletions rather than one shared deletion. This heterogeneity pointed away from a single maternally inherited mtDNA lesion and toward a nuclear gene driving the deletions.
hint: If everyone had the same band, you'd suspect one inherited mtDNA mutation—but the bands didn't line up.

q: A muscle condition was tracked through several generations of one family. Which observed transmission pattern would rule out simple maternal (mitochondrial) inheritance?
options: Affected fathers transmitted the trait, and affected mothers passed it to only some offspring
Only mothers transmitted the trait, and all of their children were affected
Only daughters were ever affected, never sons
The trait skipped every other generation regardless of parent sex
explain: Male transmission and incomplete transmission by affected females both violate strict maternal mitochondrial inheritance, in which only mothers transmit and they pass it to all offspring. These deviations implicate a nuclear (Mendelian) gene instead.
hint: Pure mtDNA inheritance is strictly maternal and passes to essentially all offspring—what two violations break that rule?

q: A muscle condition appears in every generation of a family. It is transmitted by both affected fathers and affected mothers, and affected parents pass it to about half of their children. This pattern is most consistent with which mode of inheritance?
options: Autosomal recessive
X-linked recessive
Mitochondrial (maternal)
Autosomal dominant
explain: Transmission by both males and females to about half of offspring in every generation fits autosomal dominant inheritance. Maternal-only transmission would suggest mitochondrial, and a recessive pattern would typically skip generations rather than appear in each.
hint: Both sexes transmit it and an affected parent passes it to roughly half of children—classic vertical transmission.

q: Linkage analysis showed marker H had a 93% coincidence with the disease. On which chromosome is the causative mutation most likely located?
options: Chromosome 10, because marker H showed 93% coincidence
Chromosome 21, because marker H showed 7% coincidence
The mitochondrial genome, because the deletions were in mtDNA
The X chromosome, because the trait was maternally inherited
explain: Marker H's 93% coincidence with the phenotype signals tight linkage, placing the causative nuclear gene on chromosome 10. The deletions occur in mtDNA, but the responsible mutation is in a nuclear gene, so the mitochondrial genome and a maternal-inheritance X-linkage argument are incorrect.
hint: High coincidence between a marker and the disease means tight linkage—follow marker H to its chromosome.

q: How can a mutation in a nuclear gene lead to deletions in mitochondrial DNA?
options: Nuclear genes encode proteins required for mtDNA replication and repair
Nuclear mutations directly insert into mtDNA during meiosis
Nuclear genes are physically copied into the mitochondrial genome
Mitochondria lack their own DNA and rely entirely on nuclear DNA
explain: Most proteins that replicate and repair mtDNA are encoded by nuclear genes, so a nuclear mutation in such a gene can destabilize the mitochondrial genome and cause deletions. Mitochondria do have their own DNA, and nuclear sequences are not copied or inserted into mtDNA.
hint: Mitochondria can't maintain their genome alone—where are most of their replication and repair enzymes encoded?

q: Before the mechanism of DNA replication was known, scientists proposed three competing models. Which set correctly names all three?
options: Conservative, semiconservative, dispersive
Conservative, semiconservative, semidiscontinuous
Bidirectional, unidirectional, dispersive
Continuous, discontinuous, conservative
explain: The three historical models were conservative (parent helix stays intact), semiconservative (each daughter gets one old and one new strand), and dispersive (each strand is a patchwork of old and new). Terms like 'semidiscontinuous' and 'bidirectional' describe how synthesis proceeds at a fork, not the inheritance models.
hint: Think about what happens to the parent helix: kept whole, split one-for-one, or chopped and mixed.

q: In the Meselson-Stahl experiment, cells grown in heavy ¹⁵N were shifted to light ¹⁴N. Which density-gradient result identified replication as semiconservative?
options: Every generation showed only a single band of progressively decreasing density
First generation all hybrid ¹⁵N/¹⁴N; second generation hybrid and light, with no heavy band
First generation a mix of heavy and light bands; second generation all hybrid
First generation all heavy ¹⁵N/¹⁵N; second generation a mix of heavy and light
explain: After one round all DNA was hybrid ¹⁵N/¹⁴N (a single intermediate band), ruling out conservative replication, which predicts separate heavy and light bands. After two rounds, hybrid and fully light bands appeared with no heavy band, ruling out the dispersive model, which predicts a single band of intermediate-and-decreasing density.
hint: A single hybrid band after one round kills the conservative model; the appearance of a light band after two rounds kills dispersive.

q: Under the semiconservative model, how are the two strands of the parent DNA distributed among the daughter molecules?
options: Both original strands stay together in one daughter; the other daughter is entirely new
Both original strands are degraded and replaced by two new strands
Each original strand is broken into pieces interspersed with new DNA in both daughters
Each daughter molecule receives one original strand paired with one newly made strand
explain: In semiconservative replication each parental strand serves as a template, so every daughter duplex contains one conserved old strand and one new strand. Keeping both old strands together describes the conservative model; interspersing fragments describes the dispersive model.
hint: Each parent strand acts as a template and is conserved within its own daughter duplex.

q: Why must one of the two new strands at a replication fork be synthesized discontinuously rather than as a single continuous piece?
options: DNA polymerase can add nucleotides only in the 5′→3′ direction while the two template strands run antiparallel
Helicase unwinds the helix faster than polymerase can keep pace, leaving gaps
Primase can lay down only one RNA primer per replication fork
DNA polymerase repeatedly stalls and dissociates because it lacks processivity
explain: Because the strands are antiparallel and DNA polymerase synthesizes strictly 5′→3′, the strand whose template runs 5′→3′ toward the fork must be made in short fragments pointing away from the fork (lagging strand). Processivity and helicase speed are not the cause, and primase lays many primers on the lagging strand.
hint: The fork moves one way, but polymerase can only build in one direction; one template forces synthesis to back-fill.

q: Which statement accurately describes a feature of DNA replication?
options: The lagging strand is composed of Okazaki fragments
Both new strands are synthesized in the 3′→5′ direction
Okazaki fragments are joined together by DNA polymerase III
The leading strand is built as a series of Okazaki fragments
explain: The lagging strand is assembled from short Okazaki fragments, whereas the leading strand is synthesized continuously. Fragments are sealed by DNA ligase (not polymerase III), and all synthesis proceeds 5′→3′.
hint: The strand made in pieces is the one that runs away from the fork.

q: During replication, what does the enzyme primase synthesize?
options: Single-stranded DNA-binding regions that stabilize the fork
Short segments of single-stranded DNA
Phosphodiester bonds between adjacent Okazaki fragments
Short pieces of RNA
explain: Primase is an RNA polymerase that makes the short RNA primers needed to start DNA synthesis. It does not make DNA (that is DNA polymerase) and does not seal nicks (that is ligase).
hint: DNA polymerase cannot start from scratch, so this enzyme lays down an RNA starter.

q: Synthesis of each Okazaki fragment on the lagging strand begins from what structure?
options: An RNA primer
A single-stranded DNA-binding protein anchor
An exposed 5′-phosphate at the origin
A free 3′-OH on a pre-existing DNA strand
explain: Each lagging-strand Okazaki fragment starts from an RNA primer made by primase, which supplies the 3′-OH that DNA polymerase extends. A pre-existing DNA 3′-OH is what the continuous leading strand extends from after its single primer, not how each lagging fragment is initiated.
hint: DNA polymerase needs an existing 3′-OH, and on the lagging strand that is provided by a fresh starter laid down by primase.

q: DNA polymerase can add a new nucleotide only when which feature is present on the growing strand?
options: A 3′ hydroxyl group
A free 5′-phosphate group
An exposed 2′ hydroxyl group
A blunt double-stranded end
explain: Chain elongation requires a free 3′-OH on the growing strand, which attacks the alpha-phosphate of the incoming dNTP to form the new bond, so synthesis runs 5′→3′. A 5′-phosphate cannot be extended, and the 2′ position is deoxy (no OH) in DNA.
hint: Nucleotides are added onto the growing end, attacking the incoming triphosphate; that end must carry a reactive OH.

q: Which enzyme unwinds and separates the two complementary strands of the double helix at the replication fork?
options: DNA ligase
Primase
Topoisomerase
Helicase
explain: Helicase breaks the hydrogen bonds between paired bases to separate the strands at the fork. Topoisomerase relieves the supercoiling torque ahead of the fork but does not unzip the strands; primase makes primers and ligase seals nicks.
hint: Picture the enzyme that breaks the hydrogen bonds to pry the two strands apart at the fork.

q: Which enzyme seals the nicks between adjacent fragments to produce a continuous DNA strand?
options: Helicase
DNA polymerase I
Primase
Ligase
explain: DNA ligase forms the final phosphodiester bond joining adjacent fragments into one continuous strand. DNA polymerase I removes the RNA primers and fills the gaps but leaves a nick that only ligase can seal.
hint: After primers are removed and gaps filled, one enzyme glues the pieces together.

q: In a single bidirectional replication bubble, how many replication forks form and how do they move relative to the origin?
options: One fork that moves in a single direction away from the origin
Two forks that move in opposite directions away from the origin
Two forks that move toward each other and converge on the origin
Four forks, one extending from each strand end
explain: A bidirectional bubble has two replication forks that move in opposite directions away from the single origin, enlarging the bubble. A single unidirectional fork describes a different replication mode, and forks move away from (not toward) the origin within a bubble.
hint: Bidirectional means the bubble grows outward from where it started in both directions.

q: Within a single replication bubble, which rule correctly distinguishes the leading from the lagging strands?
options: Leading strands are synthesized continuously toward the forks; lagging strands are synthesized in fragments away from the forks
Leading strands are made only on the top of the bubble; lagging strands only on the bottom
Both leading and lagging strands are synthesized continuously toward the origin
Leading strands are synthesized in fragments toward the forks; lagging strands are synthesized continuously away from the forks
explain: A leading strand is synthesized continuously in the same direction the fork moves, while a lagging strand is made discontinuously in Okazaki fragments pointing away from the fork. Position (top vs bottom) does not fix which is leading; each of the bubble's four arms is leading or lagging based on its 5′→3′ orientation.
hint: Follow the 5′→3′ rule: the strand that can chase the fork goes continuously; the other backfills in pieces.

q: What is an Okazaki fragment?
options: A length of single-stranded DNA coated by SSB proteins
A short piece of DNA synthesized on the lagging strand
A short RNA primer laid down by primase
A continuous DNA segment that makes up the entire leading strand
explain: An Okazaki fragment is a short stretch of DNA made on the lagging strand, later joined to its neighbors by ligase. The RNA primer is a separate molecule that initiates each fragment, and the leading strand is continuous rather than fragmented.
hint: It is the short DNA piece produced on the strand that is built away from the fork.

q: What is an RNA primer in the context of DNA replication?
options: A short RNA sequence added to the 3′ end of each completed Okazaki fragment
A short RNA sequence at the 5′ end of each newly synthesized DNA segment
A ribosomal RNA that recruits DNA polymerase to the template
A short DNA sequence that signals the origin of replication
explain: An RNA primer is a short RNA stretch placed at the 5′ end of each new DNA segment, providing the 3′-OH that DNA polymerase extends; it is later removed and replaced with DNA. It sits at the 5′ (start) end, not the 3′ end, and is made by primase, not from rRNA.
hint: Synthesis runs 5′→3′, so the starter sits at the beginning (5′ end) of each new DNA stretch and is later removed.

q: Which sequence correctly orders the major events of DNA replication at a fork?
options: Helicase unwinds → RNA primers added → SSB binds → ligase joins → DNA polymerase synthesizes → primers removed
RNA primers added → helicase unwinds → SSB binds → DNA polymerase synthesizes → ligase joins → primers removed
SSB binds → helicase unwinds → DNA polymerase synthesizes → RNA primers added → ligase joins → primers removed
Helicase unwinds → SSB binds → RNA primers added → DNA polymerase synthesizes → RNA primers removed → ligase joins fragments
explain: The correct order is: helicase unwinds, SSB proteins coat and stabilize the exposed single strands, primase adds RNA primers, DNA polymerase synthesizes new DNA, the RNA primers are removed and replaced, and ligase seals the fragments. Priming or ligating before the helix is unwound and coated is out of sequence.
hint: Unzip first, hold the single strands open, lay starters, build DNA, then strip the starters and stitch the pieces.

q: A circular bacterial chromosome is copied by two replication forks, each moving at 1000 nucleotides per second, and replication takes 30 minutes. How many base pairs does the chromosome contain?
explain: Each fork moves 1000 nt/s × 1800 s = 1,800,000 bp, and two forks together cover the circle: 1,800,000 × 2 = 3,600,000 bp. Using only one fork gives the 1,800,000 bp distractor.
hint: Convert minutes to seconds, multiply by the per-fork rate, then account for both forks sharing the circle.

q: DNA polymerase synthesizes 1130 bases per minute per fork, and a bacterium copies its chromosome with two forks in 40 minutes. What are the total nucleotides incorporated and the chromosome's length (0.34 nm per base pair)?
options: Total nucleotides = 180,800; length = 30,736 nm
Total nucleotides = 180,800; length = 61,472 nm
Total nucleotides = 90,400; length = 15,368 nm
Total nucleotides = 45,200; length = 30,736 nm
explain: Each fork copies 1130 × 40 = 45,200 bp, and two forks give 90,400 bp; length = 90,400 × 0.34 ≈ 30,736 nm. Total nucleotides counts both strands of those base pairs: 90,400 × 2 = 180,800.
hint: Two forks each copy 1130 × 40 bp; length uses base pairs × 0.34 nm, but total nucleotides counts both strands.

q: E. coli grown in nonradioactive phosphorus is transferred to ³²P medium. Given semiconservative replication, what is the radioactivity of the DNA immediately, after one round, and after two rounds?
options: Immediately none; after one round half the molecules fully labeled; after two rounds all fully labeled
Immediately none; after one round both strands labeled; after two rounds all molecules fully labeled
Immediately fully labeled; after one round half labeled; after two rounds none labeled
Immediately none; after one round one labeled strand per molecule; after two rounds half with one labeled strand and half fully labeled
explain: Immediately there is no ³²P. After one round each molecule has one old (unlabeled) and one new (labeled) strand. After two rounds the four molecules are half with one labeled strand (still pairing an original unlabeled strand) and half fully labeled (both strands made in ³²P).
hint: Old strands carry no label; every new strand is hot. Track each conserved parental strand through two rounds.

q: Write the complementary strand of 5′-GTATCTGCCA-3′, expressed in the conventional 5′→3′ direction.
options: 5′-TGGCAGATAC-3′
5′-TACGAGCTTG-3′
5′-CATAGACGGT-3′
5′-ACCGTCTATG-3′
explain: The complement of GTATCTGCCA read in the same direction is CATAGACGGT, and reversing it to read 5′→3′ (because strands are antiparallel) gives TGGCAGATAC. The CATAGACGGT distractor is the complement written 3′→5′, i.e. not flipped.
hint: Pair each base (A-T, G-C), then reverse the result so the antiparallel strand reads 5′→3′.

q: When drawing new-strand synthesis arrows inside a replication bubble, which rule governs their direction?
options: Arrows always point toward the origin regardless of which strand is leading or lagging
Arrows point 5′→3′; leading strands point toward the forks and lagging fragments point away from the forks
Arrows point 3′→5′; leading strands point away from the forks and lagging fragments toward the forks
Arrows point 5′→3′; both leading and lagging strands point toward the forks
explain: All synthesis arrows point 5′→3′: leading strands are drawn toward the moving forks (continuous), and lagging-strand fragments point away from the forks. Polymerase never synthesizes 3′→5′, and lagging fragments are made away from, not toward, the fork.
hint: Every arrow obeys 5′→3′; the continuous strand chases the fork while the fragmented one backfills.

q: Which replication-bubble diagram correctly depicts the strands during synthesis?
options: Leading strands continuous toward the forks, lagging strands fragmented, Okazaki fragments only on lagging strands, RNA primers at the 5′ ends of fragments
Leading strands fragmented, lagging strands continuous, Okazaki fragments only on leading strands, RNA primers at the 3′ ends of fragments
Both strands continuous toward the forks with a single RNA primer on each
Leading and lagging strands both fragmented, with RNA primers at the 3′ ends of every fragment
explain: The correct diagram shows continuous leading strands running toward the forks and fragmented lagging strands, with Okazaki fragments only on the lagging strands and RNA primers at the 5′ end of each fragment. Fragmentation on the leading strand or primers at the 3′ end violate the 5′→3′ priming rule.
hint: Continuous follows the fork; fragmented backfills; each fragment is started by a primer at its beginning (5′ end).

q: During bacterial DNA replication, which enzyme is responsible for synthesizing the short RNA primers that DNA polymerase requires to begin synthesis?
options: Single-strand binding (SSB) protein
DNA topoisomerase (gyrase)
Primase
Helicase
explain: Primase synthesizes the short RNA primers that provide a free 3'-OH for DNA polymerase. Helicase unwinds the duplex, SSB protein keeps the separated strands from reannealing, and topoisomerase/gyrase relaxes supercoiling ahead of the fork.
hint: Think about which protein 'primes' the pump by laying down RNA. Match each player to one unique job.

q: If a mutant E. coli DNA polymerase I lost only its 5'->3' exonuclease activity while retaining its other activities, what would be the most direct consequence?
options: Inability to unwind the parental double helix
Failure to remove the RNA primers
Inability to synthesize DNA to fill gaps after primer removal
Decreased proofreading and reduced replication fidelity
explain: The 5'->3' exonuclease of DNA pol I removes the RNA primers, so losing it prevents primer excision. Loss of the 3'->5' exonuclease would instead lower proofreading fidelity, and loss of the 5'->3' polymerase would block gap-filling DNA synthesis; unwinding is helicase's job, not pol I's.
hint: DNA pol I uses its 5'->3' exonuclease as the 'eraser' that chews out RNA from the front. Map each lost activity to exactly one defect.

q: An E. coli helicase strain that is resistant to the inhibitor RS2014 and also fails to grow at the restrictive (high) temperature would carry which genotype?
options: rs^R, ts^-
rs^R, ts^+
rs^S, ts^+
rs^S, ts^-
explain: Drug resistance to RS2014 is denoted rs^R, and a temperature-sensitive strain that cannot grow at the restrictive temperature is ts^-, giving rs^R, ts^-. The rs^S alleles would be drug-sensitive, and ts^+ alleles would grow normally at high temperature.
hint: Resistant to the drug = superscript R; temperature-sensitive (dies when it's hot) = ts minus. Read both phenotypes independently.

q: In prokaryotic DNA replication, which protein first binds the origin (oriC) to initiate the process before the helix is melted and unwound?
options: Tus
Primase
DnaA
DNA polymerase III
explain: DnaA is the initiator that binds oriC, triggering local melting and helicase loading; only afterward does primase lay primers and DNA polymerase synthesize new strands. Tus acts last, halting the forks at termination sites.
hint: Initiation comes first: an initiator protein lands on the origin before anything melts. Tus appears only at the very end.

q: In bacterial replication, which statement correctly describes the lagging strand and the enzymes that finish it?
options: It is synthesized discontinuously as Okazaki fragments that DNA ligase seals together
It is unwound by gyrase and stabilized by primase
It is the template that DnaA melts to form the replication bubble
It is synthesized continuously toward the fork by DNA pol III alone
explain: The lagging strand is built discontinuously as Okazaki fragments, which DNA ligase joins after pol I replaces their RNA primers; the leading strand is the continuous one. Gyrase relieves torsion and SSB (not primase) stabilizes single-stranded DNA, and DnaA initiates at the origin rather than describing the lagging strand.
hint: The lagging strand can't keep pace with the fork, so it's made in pieces. Picture who lays the pieces and who glues them.

q: Which sequence correctly orders the events of eukaryotic DNA replication?
options: Origins assemble prereplication complexes → RNA primers added → telomerase extends telomeres → DNA polymerase synthesizes strands → RNA replaced with DNA

Origins assemble prereplication complexes → initiation activates helicase/fork → RNA primers added → DNA polymerase synthesizes leading/lagging strands 5′→3′ → RNA replaced with DNA and fragments joined → telomerase extends telomeres
Telomerase extends telomeres → origins assemble prereplication complexes → RNA primers added → DNA polymerase synthesizes strands → fragments joined
RNA primers added → origins assemble prereplication complexes → DNA polymerase synthesizes strands → telomerase extends ends → RNA replaced with DNA
explain: Prereplication complexes assemble at origins first, the fork/helicase is then activated, RNA primers are laid down, polymerase extends 5′→3′, primers are replaced and Okazaki fragments ligated, and telomerase extends the chromosome ends last. Sequences that place primers or telomerase before origin licensing reverse the actual dependency order.
hint: Nothing is synthesized until the origin is licensed and the helicase is unwinding; telomere extension is a finishing step at the very end.

q: Which feature is shared by both eukaryotic and bacterial DNA replication?
options: Both replicate semiconservatively using helicase, a polymerase, and an RNA primer
Both extend chromosome ends with telomerase
Both replicate a circular chromosome located in the nucleus
Both use a single replication origin per chromosome
explain: Both systems are semiconservative and require helicase to unwind DNA, a DNA polymerase to synthesize, and an RNA primer to start. Single origin, circular chromosome, nuclear location, and telomerase are points of difference, not similarity.
hint: Focus on the universal machinery and mechanism, not on chromosome shape, number of origins, or compartment.

q: Which is a genuine difference between eukaryotic and bacterial DNA replication?
options: Eukaryotic replication is conservative, whereas bacterial replication is semiconservative
Eukaryotes replicate without a primer, whereas bacteria require an RNA primer
Eukaryotes replicate in the cytoplasm, whereas bacteria replicate in the nucleus
Eukaryotes have many origins per chromosome and linear chromosomes with telomeres, whereas bacteria have one origin on a circular chromosome
explain: Eukaryotic chromosomes are linear with telomeres and have multiple origins, replicating in the nucleus, while bacteria have a single circular chromosome with one origin. Both still use a primer and are semiconservative, and bacteria have no nucleus, so the other choices invert real facts.
hint: Think about where replication happens, how many starting points there are, the shape of the genome, and whether ends need special handling.

q: How does replication licensing restrict each origin to firing only once per cell cycle?
options: Telomerase blocks any origin that has already initiated replication
Each origin is physically destroyed after it fires so it cannot be reused
DNA polymerase methylates fired origins, permanently silencing them
Only licensed origins can initiate, and licensing components cannot reload onto fired origins until the next cell cycle
explain: Licensing permits initiation only at licensed origins, and once an origin fires its licensing/initiator components cannot be reloaded until the next cell cycle, preventing rereplication. Origins are not destroyed, methylated, or blocked by telomerase.
hint: An origin needs a 'license' to fire, and once it has fired the license can't be re-issued until the next round.

q: What G1-to-S-phase mechanism ensures a eukaryotic origin initiates replication only once?
options: Each origin is replicated by a dedicated polymerase that is consumed in the process
In G1 the ORC and licensing factors recruit an initiator/helicase complex, and after S-phase initiation the complex is prevented from rebinding DNA
Telomerase caps each origin in G1 and uncaps it after S phase
DNA ligase seals each origin after firing so it cannot reopen
explain: During G1 the origin recognition complex (ORC) and licensing factors load the initiator/helicase complex, and once S-phase initiation occurs that complex cannot rebind DNA until the next G1, blocking re-initiation. Ligase, telomerase, and consumable polymerases play no role in this control.
hint: The origin recognition complex sets things up in G1; once replication starts, the loading machinery is locked out for the rest of the cycle.

q: What underlies the end-replication problem at linear chromosome ends?
options: Helicase cannot unwind the final coils of DNA at a chromosome end
DNA polymerase proofreading removes the terminal nucleotides of each strand
DNA polymerase needs a primer and works only 5′→3′, so once the terminal RNA primer is removed there is no upstream 3′-OH to fill the resulting gap
Ligase fails to join the last Okazaki fragment to the leading strand
explain: Because DNA polymerase requires a primer and synthesizes only 5′→3′, removal of the final RNA primer at a linear end leaves a gap with no adjacent 3′-OH to prime fill-in, so the strand shortens. The cause is the polymerase's primer requirement and directionality, not helicase, proofreading, or ligase failure.
hint: The problem is on the lagging strand: removing the very last primer leaves a gap with no 3′-OH behind it to extend from.

q: Which sequence correctly orders the steps of telomerase action at a chromosome end?
options: Telomerase RNA binds 3′ overhang → telomerase released → nucleotides added to 3′ end → complementary strand filled in

Telomerase RNA binds the 3′ G overhang → nucleotides added to 3′ end → telomerase translocates toward the 3′ end → more nucleotides added → telomerase released → complementary 5′ strand synthesized to fill the gap
Nucleotides added to the 5′ end → telomerase translocates toward 5′ end → telomerase RNA binds overhang → telomerase released
Complementary 5′ strand synthesized first → telomerase RNA binds 3′ overhang → nucleotides added → telomerase released
explain: Telomerase's RNA component anneals to the 3′ G overhang, adds nucleotides to the 3′ end, translocates, adds more, then dissociates, after which conventional synthesis fills in the complementary 5′ strand. Extension is to the 3′ end first, so orderings that fill the complementary strand first or add to the 5′ end are wrong.
hint: The RNA template must dock on the G overhang before any nucleotides can be added, and the complementary strand is filled in only after the 3′ end is extended.

q: What is the expected consequence of a large deletion in the RNA component of telomerase?
options: Telomerase adds nucleotides faster, lengthening telomeres abnormally
Telomerase switches to extending the 5′ end instead of the 3′ end
Telomerase cannot properly associate with telomeres and loses the ability to synthesize new telomeric repeats
Telomerase begins replicating the entire chromosome instead of just the ends
explain: The RNA component serves as both the template for telomeric repeats and the guide for binding the telomere, so a large deletion prevents proper association and repeat synthesis. It cannot speed up extension, switch ends, or expand to whole-chromosome replication.
hint: The RNA piece is the template that recognizes the telomere; lose it and the enzyme has nothing to copy from or bind to.

q: In dyskeratosis congenita (DKC) telomere data, how do biological parents typically compare with their children?
options: Parents, on average, have longer telomeres than their children
Telomere length is unrelated between parents and children
Parents and children have telomeres of identical average length
Children, on average, have longer telomeres than their parents
explain: On average, affected biological parents have longer telomeres than their children, reflecting the progressive shortening passed across generations. Children having equal or longer telomeres would contradict the observed generational shortening.
hint: Telomeres get progressively shorter across affected generations, so the older generation started longer.

q: Why are telomeres shorter in individuals with dyskeratosis congenita (DKC)?
options: Their chromosomes lack telomeres entirely from birth
An overactive telomerase trims telomeres each cycle
Mutated telomerase cannot maintain telomeres over the person's lifetime
Their cells divide far fewer times than normal
explain: DKC involves mutated telomerase that fails to maintain telomere length, so telomeres erode over the lifetime. Telomerase is deficient rather than overactive, telomeres are present at birth, and reduced division would not shorten them.
hint: Think about which enzyme is defective and what it normally does to keep ends from eroding.

q: What best explains the phenomenon of anticipation (earlier, more severe onset across generations) in dyskeratosis congenita?
options: Each generation acquires entirely new telomerase mutations independently
Children inherit already-shortened telomeres from affected parents, and further shortening produces earlier symptoms
Symptoms appear earlier only because of improved diagnostic methods over time
Telomeres lengthen with each generation, paradoxically worsening disease
explain: Anticipation arises because children inherit telomeres that are already shortened, and continued erosion pushes them below the symptomatic threshold earlier than in their parents. New independent mutations, telomere lengthening, and diagnostic artifacts do not account for the inherited shortened baseline.
hint: Each generation starts from a shorter baseline than the last, so the threshold for symptoms is reached sooner.

q: In eukaryotes, after a replication origin fires a "licensing factor" must be removed so the origin cannot re-fire in the same cell cycle. Protein A is the factor that carries out this removal during active replication. Consistent with that role, how does the level of protein A change as a cell progresses through S phase?
options: Protein A remains at a constant level throughout the cell cycle
Protein A decreases during S phase
Protein A increases during S phase
Protein A is present only in G1 and absent in S phase
explain: Wild-type protein A increases during S phase, consistent with its role in removing the licensing factor once replication has begun. Decreasing, disappearing, or staying constant would not fit a factor that acts during active replication.
hint: Protein A is the factor whose job ramps up as replication proceeds, so its level rises when DNA is being copied.

q: After a replication origin fires, a licensing factor must be removed so the origin cannot re-fire in the same cell cycle. Protein A performs this removal. If protein A is lost by mutation, what is the most likely consequence?
options: No replication occurs at any point in the cell cycle
Telomeres fail to be extended but bulk replication is normal
Replication occurs continuously throughout the cell cycle and DNA content becomes abnormally high
Replication is restricted to a single origin only
explain: Without functional protein A the licensing factor is never removed, so origins refire continuously and DNA content rises abnormally. Loss of all replication is the mutant B phenotype, and the defect is not specific to one origin or to telomeres.
hint: If the factor that shuts re-licensing down is broken, origins keep firing and DNA piles up.

q: Protein B is a licensing factor that must be bound to a replication origin for initiation to occur, and it is cleared once replication begins so the origin cannot re-fire. Consistent with that role, how does the level of protein B change as a cell progresses through S phase?
options: Protein B increases during S phase
Protein B decreases during S phase
Protein B stays constant across the entire cell cycle
Protein B is detectable only after S phase is complete
explain: Wild-type protein B decreases during S phase, consistent with a licensing factor that is removed once replication initiates to prevent rereplication. Increasing, staying constant, or appearing only after S phase contradicts that removal.
hint: Protein B is the licensing factor that gets cleared once replication starts, so its level drops as S phase proceeds.

q: Protein B is the licensing factor that must be present at a replication origin for initiation to occur. If protein B is lost by mutation, what is the most likely consequence?
options: No replication occurs during the cell cycle
Replication proceeds continuously and DNA content rises sharply
Only telomeric regions fail to replicate
Replication speeds up but stops after one round
explain: Because protein B is the licensing factor needed for initiation, its loss means no origin can fire and no replication occurs. Continuous replication with high DNA is the mutant A phenotype, not mutant B.
hint: Protein B is the licensing factor required to start; without it, origins can never fire.

q: In a model of eukaryotic replication control, one protein licenses origins so they can initiate, and a second protein inactivates that licensing factor once replication begins to prevent re-replication. Which statement correctly describes the normal roles of proteins A and B?
options: Protein A unwinds DNA at origins while protein B proofreads the new strands
Both proteins are licensing factors that must bind simultaneously for any origin to fire
Protein A is the licensing factor for initiation, and protein B extends telomeres after replication
Protein B is a licensing factor required for initiation, and protein A removes protein B after replication starts to prevent rereplication
explain: Protein B is the licensing factor required to initiate replication, and protein A removes it once replication starts, blocking rereplication. Assigning licensing to A, telomere extension or proofreading to either, or requiring both to bind together misstates their roles.
hint: One protein is the green light to start; the other clears that green light once firing begins so origins can't refire.

q: In a replication-control system, protein B licenses origins for initiation and protein A removes that licensing factor after firing to prevent re-replication. Which combination correctly explains the phenotypes of a protein A mutant and a protein B mutant?
options: Mutant A blocks initiation so no replication occurs; mutant B leaves licensing factor in place, causing continuous replication
Both mutants independently cause continuous over-replication and elevated DNA content
Mutant A leaves the licensing factor in place, causing continuous replication and high DNA; mutant B blocks initiation, so no replication occurs
Mutant A fails to extend telomeres causing end loss; mutant B over-initiates causing high DNA
explain: Mutant A cannot remove the licensing factor, so origins refire continuously and DNA content climbs, whereas mutant B lacks the licensing factor needed to initiate, so no replication occurs. Swapping these assignments or invoking telomere loss misreads which protein does which job.
hint: Match each phenotype to its cause: failure to clear the license means endless firing; loss of the license itself means no firing at all.

q: When a Holliday junction is resolved, the strands can be cut in either of two planes. Which statement best describes the products of resolution?
options: Resolution always regenerates the two parental chromosomes with no exchange of flanking markers
Resolution always yields two crossover (recombinant) chromosomes and never leaves any heteroduplex DNA
Resolution degrades both DNA molecules into single strands that must be recopied
Cutting in one plane gives non-crossover (patch) products and cutting in the other gives crossover (splice) products, and a stretch of heteroduplex DNA can remain in either case
explain: A Holliday junction can be cleaved in two orientations: one regenerates the flanking markers as non-crossover (patch) products, while the other exchanges them as crossover (splice) products. Either way, the strand exchange leaves a region of heteroduplex DNA where the two parental strands are paired. It does not always produce crossovers, always restore parentals, or reduce the DNA to single strands.
hint: Two cut orientations, two outcomes — and the swapped single strands leave a mismatch patch behind. Think crossover vs non-crossover.

q: During homologous recombination, a stretch of duplex DNA forms in which the two strands originate from different homologous chromosomes. What is this region called?
options: Homoduplex DNA
Replicated DNA
Heteroduplex DNA
Supercoiled DNA
explain: Heteroduplex DNA is duplex DNA whose two strands come from different homologous chromosomes, formed when strands are exchanged during recombination; a homoduplex would have both strands from the same molecule, so it cannot mismatch in the way a heteroduplex can.
hint: Think 'hetero' = different sources; one strand comes from each of the two homologs after strand exchange.

q: What does the term 'Holliday junction' specifically refer to in the context of homologous recombination?
options: The double-strand break that initiates recombination
A recombination intermediate formed during homologous recombination
A region of mismatched bases repaired after strand exchange
The enzyme that cleaves and rejoins crossing DNA strands
explain: A Holliday junction is the cross-shaped recombination intermediate in which single strands are exchanged between two homologous duplexes; it is a DNA structure, not the resolvase enzyme that cuts it nor the initiating double-strand break.
hint: It is a structure, not an enzyme or a lesion; it is the crossover point linking two duplexes mid-recombination.

q: A comparative study measured average recombination rates across several species and across the different chromosomes within each species. Which conclusion does it support?
options: Recombination rate is constant across all chromosomes within a given species
Species with larger genomes always show the highest recombination rates
Humans showed the highest average recombination rate among the studied species, and rates differ among chromosomes within a species
Recombination rate can be used to directly estimate a species' genome size
explain: The data show humans had the highest average recombination rate among the studied species and that rates vary among chromosomes within a species; recombination rate does not track genome size, so inferring genome size or assuming uniform per-chromosome rates is unsupported.
hint: Stick to what the data directly show about rates; do not extrapolate to genome size, and remember rates are not uniform across chromosomes.

q: Which experimental design is NOT a valid demonstration of successful gene integration by homologous recombination in yeast?
options: Replacing a mutant essential gene with the wild-type allele in a haploid and scoring survival
Introducing TRP⁺ into a diploid carrying a single trp⁻ mutation and scoring growth on tryptophan-deficient medium
Integrating a fluorescent marker into ALT1 and detecting fluorescence
Introducing HIS⁺ into a his⁻ haploid and scoring growth on histidine-deficient medium
explain: Putting TRP⁺ into a diploid with only one trp⁻ mutation is invalid because the diploid may already carry a functional TRP allele, so growth cannot be attributed to the introduced gene; the haploid HIS⁺, ALT1 fluorescence, and essential-gene rescue designs each give a readout that only appears if integration actually occurred.
hint: Pick the setup whose readout is confounded by a pre-existing functional copy; a diploid can already carry a working allele.
